Preprint Tracker
Findings Browse GitHub
Key findings

What peer review changes — and what it does not

Highlights from a 3,010-pair analysis of bioRxiv preprints and their journal versions.

FIG 1

Study pipeline

We extracted matching preprint–published abstract pairs from bioRxiv, then labeled them with a locked codebook applied via Claude Sonnet at temperature zero. Reliability was validated against Opus and Haiku on a 425-pair subset and three independent Sonnet runs.

FIG 2

Headline rates

Two thirds of primary claims are preserved verbatim or near-verbatim. About a quarter undergo minor wording or hedging revision. Roughly 7% are substantively rewritten. The hedging dimension (right donut) is scored independently of content, so authors can soften or strengthen language without changing what they claim.

FIG 3

Content × hedging

The joint distribution shows the two dimensions behave independently: major content changes occur with all three hedging directions, and hedging shifts can occur without content change. The empty (unchanged + hedging shift) cell is impossible by codebook design — if hedging shifts, the claim is at minimum minor.

FIG 4

What changed in major-revision pairs

Among the ~7% of papers with substantive revisions, scope narrowing and outright claim replacement dominate. Direction flips and quantitative magnitude changes are rare. This means peer review most often constrains the claim's reach rather than reversing it.

FIG 5

Field-level revision rates

Rates vary substantially across bioRxiv subject areas. Some fields preserve nearly all primary claims through peer review; others see one in four claims modified. This heterogeneity matters for any reader interpreting a single paper's revisions: priors should be field-specific.

FIG 6

Retraction risk: preprinted vs not

Across 47 journals during 2018–2022 publications (n=446,273 papers), papers that had a bioRxiv preprint were retracted at 8.1 per 10,000, vs 18.7 per 10,000 for papers without one. Rate ratio 2.31× (95% CI 1.20–4.45, Fisher one-sided p = 0.003). Selection bias from author characteristics cannot be excluded, but the directional finding is consistent with the hypothesis that preprint exposure reduces post-publication retraction risk.

FIG 7

What words get added and removed

Text-mining of the primary claim across all 3,010 pairs. Words appearing in the published version but not the preprint (left) and words removed by peer review (right). The added column is dominated by hedging and softening language; the removed column shows authors editing out general framing in favor of more specific terms.

The full searchable browser lets you filter all 3,010 papers by author, journal, category, or revision label, and inspect the side-by-side primary claims for each pair.
